coredata.dc:description: As a typical pollutant in petrochemical wastewater, naphthenic acids are highly ecotoxic and difficult to biodegrade due to its robust structure. The advanced oxidation process has always been the research hotspot for naphthenic acids degradation and removal. In this work, with low cost and biomass fructose as raw material, Si-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) and its composite material (MoS2/Si-CQDs) of Flower-like molybdenum disulfide (Flower-like MoS2) were targeted synthesized by hydrothermal method, and characterized by Transmission electron microscope, Scanning electron microscope, X-Ray diffraction, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and Raman spectrum. To study the activation of prepared materials in naphthenic acid oxidative degradation, the influencing factors of kinds of materials, reaction temperature, the dosage of catalyst and persulfate were all explored systematically. The experiments showed that the naphthenic acid degradation rate of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system could reach more than 99 % in 120 min, and the relationship between the naphthenic acid degradation rate and the reaction temperature was in accordance with the quasi-primary kinetic equation. In mechanistic studies, the role of free radical and non-free radical pathways in oxidative degradation was verified. After five reuse cycles, the catalyst can still achieve a degradation rate of over 80 %. Finally, the experiment also tested the effectiveness of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system in real naphthenic acids wastewater, and the production cost of its removal of naphthenic acids was economically analyzed, showing that MoS2/Si-CQDs is a promising composite with stability and cost-effectiveness, and provides a new material for naphthenic acid degradation that can achieve a dual degradation pathway.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a typical pollutant in petrochemical wastewater, naphthenic acids are highly ecotoxic and difficult to biodegrade due to its robust structure. The advanced oxidation process has always been the research hotspot for naphthenic acids degradation and removal. In this work, with low cost and biomass fructose as raw material, Si-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) and its composite material (MoS2/Si-CQDs) of Flower-like molybdenum disulfide (Flower-like MoS2) were targeted synthesized by hydrothermal method, and characterized by Transmission electron microscope, Scanning electron microscope, X-Ray diffraction, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and Raman spectrum. To study the activation of prepared materials in naphthenic acid oxidative degradation, the influencing factors of kinds of materials, reaction temperature, the dosage of catalyst and persulfate were all explored systematically. The experiments showed that the naphthenic acid degradation rate of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system could reach more than 99 % in 120 min, and the relationship between the naphthenic acid degradation rate and the reaction temperature was in accordance with the quasi-primary kinetic equation. In mechanistic studies, the role of free radical and non-free radical pathways in oxidative degradation was verified. After five reuse cycles, the catalyst can still achieve a degradation rate of over 80 %. Finally, the experiment also tested the effectiveness of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system in real naphthenic acids wastewater, and the production cost of its removal of naphthenic acids was economically analyzed, showing that MoS2/Si-CQDs is a promising composite with stability and cost-effectiveness, and provides a new material for naphthenic acid degradation that can achieve a dual degradation pathway.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Naphthenic acids are natural constituents of crude oil, accounting for about 2–4 % wt of the average composition of crude oil [1]. Under natural conditions, most naphthenic acids are dark brown viscous oily liquid with low volatility and relatively stable properties. In the present study, naphthenic acids are generally divided into two types according to the different molecular composition: classical naphthenic acids and oxidized Naphthenic acids [2]. Classical naphthenic acids are represented by the general formula C n H 2n+Z O2, where n represents the number of carbon atoms and Z is zero or a negative even number, denoting the hydrogen that has lost its covalent bond due to the presence of the ring structure [3–5]. Oxidized naphthenic acids are described by the general formula C n H2n+Z O x , where x ranges from 2 to 5 [2,6,7].

As a class of highly toxic and difficult-to-degrade compounds unique to petroleum and petrochemical industry wastewater, the efficient removal of naphthenic acid pollutants has always been an important research direction and engineering hotspot in the field of water pollution control [8]. Naphthenic acids have strong ecotoxicity [9]. According to relevant literature review, it was found that when the concentration of Naphthenic acids in water reaches 2.5–5 mg·L−1, it will affect the normal survival of fish [10]. When the concentration of naphthenic acids reaches 300 mg·Kg−1, it will cause significant cardiovascular disease and liver disease in mice [11]. With the development of the petrochemical industry, the pollution problem of Naphthenic acids can no longer be avoided. However, naphthenic acids are difficult to volatilize and degrade [12,13], which makes it difficult to treat the wastewater containing naphthenic acids. If the produced water or industrial wastewater treatment is incomplete or not up to standard, naphthenic acids will accumulate into environment with the discharge of extracted water and cause serious pollution.

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are a technology to quickly and effectively treat organic pollutants in wastewater by using in situ generated strong oxidizing radicals (hydroxyl radicals (•OH), sulfate radicals (SO4 •−), and superoxide radicals (O2 •−)) [9]. AOPs can completely mineralize naphthenic acids by destroying their molecular structure, so AOPs have received extensive attention in Naphthenic acids treatment technology selection [14,15]. AOPs include a number of different processes such as photolysis, UV/H2O2, UV/Chlorine, ozonation, photocatalysis, Fenton-based processes, electrochemical oxidation processes, sulfate radical based processes and others [9]. Among them, the persulfate oxidation technology based on SO4 •− is a similar oxidation system to Fenton, in which the active component SO4 •− is mainly generated by the activation of persulfate. The researchers concluded that SO4 •− has a higher redox potential (2.5–3.1 V), longer half-life (30–40 μs), wider pH applicability range, lower storage and transportation costs than •OH [14,16,17]. These properties allow SO4 •− to have better oxidizability, stability and applicability for pollution remediation in various water environments, and having a wider range of applications in naphthenic acids degradation [18–21]. However, persulfate has low activity and is not easy to react directly with organic pollutants, so a variety of external methods are usually required to activate oxidizing active substances to degrade organic pollutants. The common methods of persulfate activation include energy (UV, thermal, ultrasonic, microwave) activation, electrochemical activation, alkali activation, transition metal activation, and activation with carbon-based materials, etc [14,22]. As a typical transitional metal dichalcogenides, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a potential material for the activation of NaPS due to its special layered stacking structure, abundant active sites and unsaturated edges, high electron mobility and environmental compatibility [23–25].

It has been reported that the introduction of carbon-based materials into transition metal compounds to form composite catalysts can further improve the catalytic activity and electron transfer efficiency of the composite catalysts [26]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of carbon particles less than 10 nm in size with abundant surface functional groups and a lattice structure similar to that of graphitized carbon, usually consisting of a sp2/sp3 carbon core and an outer layer of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups [27–29]. It was found that carbon-based materials with sp2 hybridized structures can act as electronic mediators to promote the degradation and oxidation of pollutants in the field of persulfate oxidation, and play a key role in inducing the electron transfer process [30,31]. As zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs with abundant surface functional groups can easily interact strongly with MoS2 through surface functional groups, affecting the surface structure and charge distribution of MoS2/CQDs composites [32]. Meanwhile, originating from the high electron mobility of MoS2 and the unique electron transfer property of CQDs, the MoS2/CQDs composites can have higher electron transfer rate, and the correlation between the two materials is conducive to the charge transfer in the activation process of persulfate, which accelerates the generation of the active substances (SO4 •−, •OH, et al.) in the oxidation reaction system, thus improving the oxidative degradation efficiency of the target pollutants [33]. In addition, the outstanding electron-transferring properties may also enhance the efficiency of non-radical reactions in the reaction system, further accelerating the degradation of pollutants.

As mentioned in the first paragraph, although the description of naphthenic acids structure has been expanded to include a wider range of functional groups and other heteroatoms, most of the strongly ecotoxic Naphthenic acids still belong to the classical naphthenic acids, i.e., “O2”- naphthenic acids [5]. Therefore, 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid (C7H10O2) was chosen as the main experimental object and a composite material (MoS2/Si-CQDs) of Flower-like MoS2 and Si-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) was designed, which was used to activate sodium persulfate (NaPS) for oxidative degradation of C7H10O2 (MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system). The novelty of this paper lies in the synergistic combination of Si-CQDs and MoS2 through strong C-O-Mo interfacial interactions, which enhances electron transfer and enables a dual degradation pathway: radical-based oxidation via reactive oxygen species and non-radiative electron transfer. The catalytic properties of MoS2/Si-CQDs were investigated by Transmission electron microscope, scanning electron microscope and X-Ray diffraction characterization, and the effects of parameters such as material dosage, temperature and sodium persulfate dosage on the degradation of naphthenic acids were determined. In addition, the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was tested for the degradation of real wastewater containing naphthenic acids, the reusability of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS under optimal experimental conditions was evaluated as well as cost analysis. Finally, the main degradation pathways were explored and the degradation mechanisms were analyzed by electron paramagnetic resonance and electrochemical means.

Naphthenic acids (3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid) and CuSO4·5 H2O were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiourea and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium persulfate, D-Fructose, (Lauryldimethylammonio) acetate, tetraethyl orthosilicate, ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate， and pyridine, tertbutyl alcohol (TBA)，methyl alcohol(MeOH), 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The purity of (Lauryldimethylammonio)acetate was 30 %. All reagents were used as received without further purification. Two typical EDWs were sampled from two oil refineries located in Qinhuangdao (Hebei Province, China) and Huizhou (Guangdong province, China), respectively. The two samples of EDWs were named as QHD-EDW and HZ-EDW, respectively.

Deep eutectic solvent (DES) is a new type of ionic liquid consisting of hydrogen-bond donor (HBD, such as acid, alcohol, amine, etc.) and hydrogen-bond acceptor (HBA, such as quaternary ammonium salt) connected by hydrogen bonding, which has the property of being structurally designable, and thus is often used as a precursor for the preparation of CQDs [34,35]. In this experiment, a Si-doped DES (Si-DES) was firstly prepared and Si-CQDs was prepared on this basis.

8.14 g (Lauryldimethylammonio)acetate and 5.40 g D-Fructose were mixed in a 100 mL three-necked flask at the molar ratios of 1:1. The mixture was heated and stirred at 90 °C for 3 h to obtain Fructose-Deep eutectic solvent，which was a known green precursor for CQDs. 0.60 g tetraethyl orthosilicate was weighed into 3.60 g Fructose-Deep eutectic solvent according to 1:6 mass ratio, then the mixture was heated and stirred at 90 °C for 30 min to obtain Si-DES.

Si-CQDs were prepared on the basis of Si-DES. 0.5 g Si-DES and 30 mL deionized water were added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the mixed solution was heated for 12 h at 200 ℃ at a heating rate of 5 ℃/min. The product was cooled to room temperature and then filtered through 0.22 µm water or organic filter membrane to obtain the Si-CQDs.

Flower-like MoS2 was prepared by a modified procedure [36]. Typically, 1 mmol ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4 H2O, 1.236 g) and 30 mmol thiourea (2.283 g) were dissolved in deionized water (35 mL) under vigorous stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 220 °C. After 18 h, the reaction system was allowed to cool to room temperature. The final product was centrifuged at 4 000 rpm for 20 min to obtain black precipitate. The black precipitate was washed with water and absolute ethanol for several times to remove any possible ions, and drying with vacuum freeze dryer to obtain Flower-like MoS2.

0.3 g Flower-like MoS2 and 30 mL Si-CQDs were added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 100 °C. After 18 h, the reaction system was allowed to cool to room temperature. The final product was centrifuged at 4 000 rpm for 10 min to obtain black precipitate. The black precipitate was washed with water and absolute ethanol for several times to remove any possible ions, and drying with vacuum freeze dryer to obtain MoS2/Si-CQDs.

The materials surface was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) using Al Kα radiation (hv=1486.6 eV). The binding energy was also calibrated based on C 1 s at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-6100S UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Metash instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette. The morphology and nanostructure of the materials were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (TESCAN MIRA LMS, CZ) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Thermo Fisher Talos F200s, USA). The crystalline phases of the materials were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Corporation D/Max-2500, Japan). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained at 77 K using an automated analyzer (Quantachrome Autosorb-iQ, USA). Prior to measurement, samples were desorbed at 433 K for 4 h to remove residual moisture and volatiles. The model of instrument used for Raman spectrum HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) tests were performed on DSC/TGA-MS/IR STA449 F5-QMS403D (Netzsch Inc., USA) equipment. 10 ± 1 mg of catalyst was taken, N2 was used as a protective gas (50 mL·min−1), air was used as the test atmosphere (20 mL·min−1), tested in the temperature range of 50–1000 ℃ (10 ℃·min−1). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra was recorded on a Bruker EMXplus-6/1 spectrometer, with a central magnetic field of 3350 G, a modulation amplitude of 1 G, and a scan time of 30 s, identification tests for •OH and SO4 •−: were carried out using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrole-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trapping agent. Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) measurement and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out in a DH7003 electrochemical workstation (DONGHUA, China).

Referring to previous studies, the spectrophotometric method was used to quantify naphthenic acids according to the Lambert-Beer law [37]. CuSO4·5 H2O (0.1 mol·L−1) solution, pyridine solution (4 mol·L−1), and a series of Naphthenic acids standard solutions with concentration gradients (0.015 mol·L−1, 0.01 mol·L−1, 0.0075 mol·L−1, 0.005 mol·L−1, 0.0025 mol·L−1, 0.001 mol·L−1, 0.0005 mol·L−1, 0.0001 mol·L−1, 0.00005 mol·L−1) were formulated. After mixing 0.5 mL CuSO4·5 H2O solution, 0.5 mL pyridine solution and 3 mL Naphthenic acids standard solution, the absorbance of the mixed solution at 600 nm was measured. A linear fit was performed with absorbance as the vertical coordinate and naphthenic acids solution concentration as the horizontal coordinate, and with the limit of detection determined. The standard curve is shown in Fig S1.

Sodium persulfate (NaPS) was selected as the oxidant and MoS2/Si-CQDs as the catalyst. 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid (C7H10O2) was chosen as the model naphthenic acids for the standard curve and subsequent oxidative degradation experiments. In the following descriptions of naphthenic acids, “Naphthenic acids” or “naphthenic acids” represents “3- Cyclohexenecarboxylic” unless otherwise indicated.

The effects of temperature, catalyst type and dosage, and oxidant dosage on the efficiency of oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system were investigated. The synergistic effects of radical and non-radical (electron transfer) in the oxidative degradation system were verified by EPR tests and electrochemical experiments. In addition, in order to study the reactive radicals that play a role in the degradation process, certain amounts of the radical scavengers methanol (MeOH) and tert-butanol (TBA) were added to the reaction, and 1,4 -Benzoquinone (BQ) was used as a bursting agent for O2 •−. For stability testing, the material was washed three times with ethanol and water and dried in a 333 K oven and repeated five times.
Specific information on the above experiments is shown in Text S1 to S6 in the Supplementary Data.
0.04 g MoS2/Si-CQDs and 0.08 g NaPS were added to 20 mL QHD-EDW and reacted at 70 ℃ for 2 h. Similarly, 0.08 g MoS2/Si-CQDs and 0.16 g NaPS were added to 40 mL HZ-EDW and reacted at 70 ℃ for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Si-CQDs and Flower-like MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 show the successful preparation of Si-CQDs. Si-CQDs are spherical in appearance, and the particle size is relatively uniform, mainly concentrated in the range of 1–3 nm, with an average particle size of 1.72 nm (Fig. 1), and the three elements of C, O, and Si were uniformly distributed on the surface of Si-CQDs (Fig. 2). The carbon structure of Si-CQDs was characterized using raman spectroscopy as shown in Fig S2. The characteristic peak centered at 1357 cm−1 belongs to the D band, and the D peak corresponds to the sp3 defect in Si-CQDs; the characteristic peak centered at 1572 cm−1 belongs to the G band, and the G peak is related to the in-plane stretching vibration of sp2 carbon, suggesting the presence of aromatic groups inside the Si-CQDs. The calculated combined intensity ratio ID/IG of peak D and peak G is 0.886, which indicates that a defective graphitic carbon phase was formed at the carbon sites during the hydrothermal process, Si-CQDs are more graphitized and contain more crystalline carbon than amorphous carbon [38,39].

As shown in Fig. 3(a), (b) and (c), the lamellar MoS2 grows in radially ordered stacks from a center in all directions by self-assembly, closely connected to form Flower-like MoS2. XRD (Fig. 3(d)) showed that the diffraction peaks of Flower-like MoS2 were in good agreement with the standard profile of 2H-MoS2 (JCPDS card No. 37–1492). The main diffraction peaks 14.1°, 33.2°, 39.6°, and 58.6° corresponded to the (002), (100), (103), and (110) crystal planes of 2H-MoS2 in turn, and there were no other diffraction peaks, which indicated that the sample was a high-purity 2H-MoS2 [36].

There is obvious broadening of all the peaks in the diffraction pattern, which suggests the nanoscale of the crystallites in different dimensions [40]. In addition, the lamella thickness of Flower-like MoS2 was calculated to be 6.0 nm according to Scherrer's formula, which is consistent with the findings in the literature [36]. In Fig. 3(e), the characteristic peaks at positions 378 cm−1 and 405 cm−1 represent the vibrations of in-plane E2 g 1 and out-of-plane A1 g, respectively. The in-plane E2 g 1 vibrational mode results from the opposite vibrations of the two S atoms with respect to the Mo atoms, while the A1 g vibrational mode is associated with the out-of-plane vibration of only the S atoms in the opposite direction. The vibrations represented by these two Raman characteristic peaks are typical Raman vibrational modes of MoS2, further confirming the formation of the pure MoS2 phase and indicating the successful synthesis of 2H-MoS2 [41,42].
Before catalysis tests, the morphological information and structure of MoS2/Si-CQDs were revealed by SEM, TEM and other techniques.

As shown in Fig S3, the macroscopic morphology and surface element distribution of MoS2/Si-CQDs were characterized by SEM. Fig S3 (a) and (b) show the morphologies of MoS2/Si-CQDs at 1 μm and 100 nm scales, respectively. It can be seen that the main body of MoS2/Si-CQDs consists of a large number of lamellar MoS2 stacked in close proximity. Compared with Flower-like MoS2 in Fig. 3, the subjective morphology of MoS2/Si-CQDs has undergone almost no new changes. The distribution of surface elements of MoS2/Si-CQDs at macroscopic (1 μm scale) was tested (Fig S3(c)-(h)). The test results showed that the Si-CQDs were uniformly loaded on Flower-like MoS2.

Fig. 4(a) shows the morphology of MoS2/Si-CQDs under TEM. The TEM image of MoS2/Si-CQDs exhibit a thick center and thin edges, which corresponds to the SEM results. Fig. 4(b) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of MoS2/Si-CQDs, and Fig. 4(c) is obtained by zooming the diffraction center. The SAED image of MoS2/Si-CQDs showing three different crystallographic planes: (110) facets (0.16 nm) and (100) facets (0.27 nm) attributed to 2H-MoS2; and (100) facets (0.21 nm) attributed to graphitic carbon, which is the characteristic crystallographic planes of CQDs. The SAED results confirm the coexistence of MoS2 and Si-CQDs in MoS2/Si-CQDs [32,43].

The distribution of elemental species on the surface of the “petals” of MoS2/Si-CQDs was tested using the energy spectrometer (EDS) in conjunction with the TEM, and the content of each element was analyzed and counted. Fig. 4(d)-(i) show the distribution of four elements, C, Si, S, and Mo, on the surface of MoS2/Si-CQDs, respectively. All the four elements are clearly and uniformly distributed on the surface of the material, indicating that the Si-CQDs are well dispersed on the surface of MoS2. Fig S4 shows the EDS test results of four elements, C, Si, S, and Mo, on the surface of MoS2/Si-CQDs, and the percentages are summarized in Table S1. The atomic fractions of C, Si, S, and Mo are 21.17 %, 0.86 %, 51.79 %, and 26.18 %, respectively, and the ratio of the atomic fractions of S and Mo is 2:1, which is consistent with the structural formula of MoS2.

Fig. 5(a) shows the comparison of XRD spectra of MoS2 and MoS2/Si-CQDs. In addition to the four characteristic peaks of MoS2, the XRD spectrum of MoS2/Si-CQDs shows a new characteristic peak at 41.3°, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite and belongs to Si-CQDs [44,45]. It agrees with the result of SAED, demonstrating the loading of Si-CQDs on the MoS2 surface. Raman spectroscopy tests (Fig. 5(b)) show two distinct peaks at 378 cm−1 and 405 cm−1 for MoS2/Si-CQDs, corresponding to the in-plane low-energy E2 g 1 vibrational mode and the out-of-plane high-energy A1 g vibrational mode of MoS2, respectively. In addition to the characteristic peaks at 378 cm−1 and 405 cm−1 attributed to MoS2, Raman spectra of MoS2/Si-CQDs also show the D peak (1357 cm−1) and G peak (1572 cm−1) attributed to Si-CQDs, respectively. The results of XRD and Raman spectroscopy tests showed that the doping of Si-CQDs did not change the crystal structure of Flower-like MoS2.

The TG-DSC variation curves of Flower-like MoS2 and MoS2/Si-CQDs are shown in Fig S5. As can be seen from Fig S5(a), there are a total of three stages of weight loss (black lines) of Flower-like MoS2 in the range of 50–1000 ℃ under air atmosphere, and the corresponding DSC changes are shown in the red curve in Fig S5(a). Fig S5(b) shows the TG-DSC variations of MoS2/Si-CQDs, which also show three distinct weight loss stages. Differently, the DSC of MoS2/Si-CQDs in the first weight loss phase shows two different scenarios compared to Fig S5(a). The mild weight loss phase below 300 °C shown by DSC of Fig S5(b) may be due to the evaporation of water molecules from the sample and the removal of unstable oxygen-containing functional groups from the carbon surface; the weight loss in the range of 300–436 °C and the appearance of shoulder peaks may be due to the decomposition and combustion of amorphous carbon and Si-CQDs in MoS2/Si-CQDs. The DSC peak at 469 °C is the same as the DSC peak at 400 °C in Fig S5(a), which is attributed to Flower-like MoS2. After 436 °C, the TG and DSC changes of MoS2/Si-CQDs in Fig S5(b) are the same as those of Flower-like MoS2 in Fig S5(a), suggesting that the thermal decomposition of Si-CQDs in MoS2/Si-CQDs are mainly concentrated in the first weight-loss stage. At the end of the first weight loss stage, the mass of Flower-like MoS2 is 85.11 % of the initial mass and the mass of MoS2/Si-CQDs are 75.68 % of the initial mass. The calculated mass difference of 9.43 % is the mass content of Si-CQDs in MoS2/Si-CQDs.

In general, the larger the specific surface area of a catalyst, the greater its catalytic activity. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the BJH pore size distributions of Flower-like MoS2 and MoS2/Si-CQDs are shown in Fig S6 and Fig S7, respectively. According to Fig S6, it can be observed that the isotherms of Flower-like MoS2 and MoS2/Si-CQDs display type IV isotherm with a typical H3 hysteresis loop [46]. It implies that both Flower-like MoS2 and MoS2/Si-CQDs are mesoporous materials [19]. However, it is interesting to note that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig S6), BJH pore size distributions (Fig S7), specific surface area (SBET) and pore volume (Table S2) of MoS2/Si-CQDs were smaller than those of Flower-like MoS2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of naphthenic acids in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different reaction systems on the degradation of naphthenic acids was firstly investigated ( Fig. 6(a), Fig S8). It can be seen that at the reaction temperature of 70 ℃ (Fig. 6a), the degradation degree of the four reaction systems on Naphthenic acids is MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS (98.60 %)＞Flower-like MoS2@NaPS (79.86 %)＞NaPS (16.95 %)＞MoS2/Si-CQDs (9.82 %). Meanwhile, the degradation of Naphthenic acids by Si-CQDs@NaPS system was 44.69 % at 80 °C (Fig S8), which was also lower than that of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system.

Further, on the basis of the above experiments, the catalytic performance of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system on naphthenic acids degradation and the corresponding kinetic behaviors were investigated with various reaction parameters (e.g. temperature, the dosage of catalyst and persulfate).

The effects of temperature were investigated as shown in Fig. 6(c). At 40 ℃, the degradation rate of naphthenic acids was 84.40 % within 120 min. When the temperature was increased to 70 ℃ and 80 ℃, the 120 min degradation rates of naphthenic acids were 98.60 % and 99.11 %, respectively, the degradation efficiency was almost constant from 70°C to 80°C, in order to reduce the energy consumption, the subsequent experiments were carried out at 70°C. Pseudo-first-order kinetics based on Langmuir–Hinshelwood model (Eq. (1)) was used to fit the naphthenic acids degradation curves [47,48], as can be seen from Fig S9a, the efficiency of the degradation reaction increases with increasing temperature, with k
obs increasing from 0.01586 min−1 to 0.03768 min−1, with a good fit and R2 above 0.99, consistent with quasi-primary kinetics. The pseudo-first-order rate constant, k
obs , was obtained by linear regression of Eq. (2), which was derived from Eq. (1) when t = 0, C = C 0:
(1) -dC/dt = kobs C
(2) ln(C/C0) = -kobs t
where C 0 and C are the initial naphthenic acids concentration and the concentration at time t, respectively. It could be concluded that NaPS activation processes were improved with elevated temperatures as k
obs increased remarkably with increasing temperature (Fig. S9(a) and Fig. 6(d)). Two reasons maybe could account for this phenomenon. On one hand, higher temperature could accelerate the decomposition of NaPS and therefore facilitate the degradation of naphthenic acids, as increasing temperature enhanced the removal rate of naphthenic acids by NaPS alone (Fig. S9(b)). On the other hand, electron transfer between naphthenic acids and NaPS was promoted with elevated temperature, which promotes the degradation process. The activation energy of naphthenic acids degradation on MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS was evaluated to be 20.33 kJ·mol−1 using the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (3); Fig. S9(c)).
(3)
ln
k obs
=
−
E
a
RT
+ ln
A
where R is the molar gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1), E a is the apparent activation energy, and A is the pre-exponential factor.

As the amount of MoS2/Si-CQDs increased, naphthenic acids removal increased from 98.60 % (0.02 g) to 99.46 % (0.06 g) within 120 min (Fig. 6(e)), and the corresponding k
obs increased from 0.03405 min−1 to 0.04343 min−1 (Fig. S10 and Fig. 6(f)). It is because MoS2/Si-CQDs acted as an activator and provided more active sites, which enhanced naphthenic acids degradation efficiency was realized. As shown in Fig. 6(g), Fig. 6(h) and Fig. S11 when NaPS dosage increased from 0.04 g to 0.08 g, naphthenic acids degradation rate increased from 98.29 % to 99.42 %, the k
obs increased from 0.03326 min−1 to 0.04257 min−1. This was mainly because large doses of NaPS can provide more reactive free radicals for the reaction [49]. However, when NaPS dosage increased from 0.08 g to 0.1 g, naphthenic acids degradation rate reduced to 99.40 %, and the k
obs decreased from 0.04257 min−1 to 0.04234 min−1, almost no significant changes in degradation efficiency and kinetic constants. It might be ascribed to the self-quenching effect among excessive radicals, in detail, there is an optimum amount of persulfate above which degradation is inhibited by the reaction of excess persulfate with sulfate radicals (Eq. (4)) reducing the effectiveness organic oxidation [22,50].
(4) SO4 •−+S2O8 2− → SO4 2−+S2O8 2•−

The solution photographs of naphthenic acids degradation by MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system at different reaction times are shown in Fig S12. The original naphthenic acids solution was in an emulsion state, and after the start of the oxidation reaction, the solution had become clear and translucent at 60 min and even clearer at 120 min as the reaction time progressed. The visual comparison between 0 min and 120 min solutions is shown in Fig. 6(g) inset.

In addition to C7H10O2 acid, Cyclohexanecarboxylic acid (C7H12O2), Benzoic acid (C7H6O2) and 3-Cyclohexylpropionic acid (C9H16O2) were also selected as auxiliary experiments to investigate the general applicability of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system. 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid, Cyclohexanecarboxylic acid and Benzoic acid were chosen as model analogs for olefinic, alkane, and aromatic naphthenic acids contaminants in oilfield extracted water, respectively [51]. The relevant experimental results are shown in Table S4. It can be seen that MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system has a good degradation effect on all four typical naphthenic acids.

Table 1 compares the processing efficiency as well as time of MoS2/Si-CQDs with various materials in the literature for the naphthenic acid model, from which it is evident that the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system is more efficient and faster for the naphthenic acid model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action: radical and non-radical synergy
ce:section.ce:para:
In this paper, bursting experiments were carried out on the main radicals generated in the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system. Usually, •OH and SO4 •− are the reactive substances for contaminant oxidation in NaPS activation, and tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) and methanol (MeOH) are generally used as free radicals quenching agents, and MeOH can effectively scavenge SO4 •− and •OH with comparable reaction MeOH can effectively scavenge SO4 •− and •OH with comparable reaction rates, whereas TBA only exhibits high reactivity towards •OH [57]. As can be seen in Fig. 7(a), the presence of MeOH and TBA inhibited the degradation of naphthenic acids, with K
obs decreasing from 0.03559 min−1 to 0.00895 min−1 and 0.00608 min−1. In addition, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) was added to determine the presence of superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−), and as can be seen in Fig. 7(b), BQ also had a slight inhibitory effect on the reaction, with a decrease in the K
obs from 0.03559 min−1 to 0.01447 min−1, which suggests that O2 •− is also involved in the degradation of naphthenic acids. These scavenging results confirm that SO4 •−, •OH and O2 •− and all contribute to the degradation of naphthenic acids in the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system, where •OH is the dominant radical. The above results indicate that the reaction system is not completely inhibited, suggesting that the system may still be affected by non-radical pathways. MoS2/Si-CQDs effectively activates persulfate, generating reactive substances such as •OH and SO4 •−, which can effectively promote the degradation of naphthenic acids.

The mechanism of naphthenic acids degradation in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was investigated. Generally, in AOPs, reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by the oxidant in the presence of the catalyst is the main factor contributing to the mineralization of pollutants [58]. Fig. 8 shows EPR spectra of reactive radicals generated by MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system. Identification of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-SO4 •− adducts in the EPR spectra validated the generation of •OH and SO4 •− in the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system (Fig. 8(a)). The peak strength of •OH is higher and that of SO4 •− is weaker. It is important to note here that the signal strength of the DMPO-SO4 •− adducts and DMPO-•OH adducts do not represent their relative abundance in the system. It is due to the fact that although SO4 •− generated during the oxidation reaction can react with DMPO to form DMPO-SO4 •− adducts, the short half-life of DMPO-SO4 •− in the aqueous environment (t 1/2 = 95 s) results in the ability of rapid nucleophilic substitution of DMPO-SO4 •− to produce DMPO-•OH during the operation of EPR (Eq. (5))[59]. In addition, as shown in Eq. (6), during the evolution of radicals, part of SO4 •− will be converted to •OH, which will lead to the reduction of SO4 •− in the oxidation process. In addition to the above reasons involving radical conversion, another reason is because the capture efficiency of DMPO for •OH is higher than that for SO4 •− during the process of radical capture, resulting in the peak intensity of the DMPO-SO4 •− adduct being much lower than that of DMPO-•OH [58,60–62].
(5) DMPO-SO4 •− + H2O → DMPO-•OH + H+ + SO4 2−
(6) SO4 •− + H2O → •OH + H+ + SO4 2−

It has been shown that superoxide radicals (HO2 •/O2 •−) can also be produced together with SO4 •− and •OH during the activation of persulfate (Eqs. (7)-(10)), which can also have an effect on the degradation reaction. In addition, O2 •− may also act as key intermediates in the evolution of other active substances (Eq. (11))[58].
(7) •OH + OH− → O•− + H2O
(8) •OH + O•− → HO2 −
(9) •OH + HO2 − → OH− + HO2 •
(10) S2O8 2− + HO2 − → SO4 2− + SO4 •− + O2 •− + H+
(11) S2O8 2− + O2 •− → SO4 2− + SO4 •− + O2

After shielding the EPR signals of •OH and SO4 •− with absolute methanol as the reaction medium, the EPR spectrum of O2 •− was obtained (Fig. 8(b)). It is shown that O2 •− was generated in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system.

As a typical non-radical reactive oxygen species in persulfate oxidation technology, singlet oxygen (1O2) formation in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was also investigated. The test results, as shown in Fig S13, showed that there was no obvious signal in the EPR spectra of 1O2. Therefore, there was no involvement of 1O2 in the degradation of naphthenic acids in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system.

Meanwhile, as shown in Fig S14 and Table S3, the total spin numbers and spin concentration of •OH and SO4 •− in MoS2@NaPS system are higher than MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system under the same conditions, the test results are mutually confirmed with BET.

Interestingly, some researchers have found that in addition to the role of ROS-based radical in the advanced oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids, there is also a non-radical role - electron transfer [49,63,64]. Therefore, whether there is also a non-radical effect based on electron transfer in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system, which jointly promotes the removal of naphthenic acids with ROS, is a question worthy of discussion.

As speculated above, on the basis of radical experiments, the mechanism of non-radical action based on electron transfer in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was further verified. The electron transfer effect in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was verified by LSV and EIS experiments. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the current increase with the addition of naphthenic acids or NaPS alone was minor in the scanned voltage ranges, indicates that the electron transfer at the electrode interface is very slow. When NaPS and naphthenic acids were both added, an obvious oxidation peak along with a significant current increase could be observed. This observation suggests that close interaction of both NaPS and naphthenic acids to MoS2/Si-CQDs surface is essential to facile electron transfer from naphthenic acids to NaPS. Impedance spectroscopic analysis demonstrated that the modification of Flower-like MoS2 with Si-CQDs remarkably reduced the charge transfer resistance at the MoS2-based activator/electrolyte interface in the presence of naphthenic acids and NaPS (Fig. 9(b)), due to the good electrical conductivity of MoS2/Si-CQDs, the resistance of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS is significantly reduced and the EIS plot is basically straight. This indicates that the electron transfer performance of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system is indeed superior to MoS2@NaPS system, and the system accelerated the oxidation reaction and rapid electron transfer on the surface of MoS2/Si-CQDs, which could be used as an electron transfer medium to promote electron transfer from organic matter to NaPS, leading to oxidative degradation independent of free radicals [65]. The EIS results were attributed to the stabilized interface and effective charge transfer of MoS2/Si-CQDs, validating the mechanism of the synergistic action of ROS-based radicals and electron transfer-based non-radicals to degrade cycloalkanoic acid in the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system.

The MoS2/Si-CQDs before and after the degradation reaction were characterized by XPS. The characterization results are shown in Fig S15 and Fig. 10, “Fresh” means before the reaction and “Used” means after the reaction. First, as shown in Fig S15, the XPS full spectrum of MoS2/Si-CQDs confirmed the co-existence of C, O, S and Mo before and after the reaction process. Fig. 10 shows the fine spectrum of each element. For fresh MoS2/Si-CQDs, C 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 284.70, 286.07, and 289.1 eV (Fig. 10(a)), which can be associated with C-C/CC, C-O, and CO bonds, respectively [66–68]. It corresponds to the C 1 s spectrum of Si-CQDs (Fig S16) [69,70]. O 1 s spectrum was also deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 530.79, 531.95, and 533.12 eV (Fig. 10(b)), which can be associated with CO, C-O-Mo, and C-OH, respectively demonstrating the formation of C–O–Mo bonds in MoS2/Si-CQDs [68], It indicates that Si-CQDs and Flower-like MoS2 combine to form MoS2/Si-CQDs through strong interfacial interaction[71]. Meanwhile, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups provided MoS2/Si-CQDs with excellent electrical conductivity and surface wettability, which provided the basis for the excellent catalytic activation and degradation exhibited by MoS2/Si-CQDs in aqueous environment. Fig. 9(c) shows the S 2p spectrum of fresh MoS2/Si-CQDs, The two characteristic peaks located at binding energies 162.25 eV and 163.53 eV are attributed to S2- 2p3/2 and S2- 2p1/2 [72,73], respectively. The presence of a large amount of unsaturated sulfur in MoS2/Si-CQDs facilitates the improvement of catalytic activity [67]. Mo 3d spectrum of fresh MoS2/Si-CQDs (Fig. 10(d)) exhibited five fitted characteristic peaks at 229.45 eV (Mo4+ 3d5/2), 232.58 eV (Mo4+ 3d3/2), 232.47 eV (Mo6+ 3d5/2), 235.53 eV (Mo6+ 3d3/2, generally corresponds to Mo-O [73]), and 226.69 eV (assigned to the S 2 s of divalent sulfide ions) [73,74]. The coexistence and transformation of Mo4+ and Mo6+ in MoS2/Si-CQDs can accelerate the transfer of electrons during the reaction process, thus accelerating the production of active substances in the oxidation system [67]. Compared to the high-resolution Mo 3d and S 2 s spectra of fresh MoS2/Si-CQDs, the binding energies of used MoS2/Si-CQDs shifted toward high-energy direction (Fig. 10(c) and (d)), indicating a change in the electronic structure and electron density loss from MoS2/Si-CQDs [68,75]. The increase in the binding energies of Mo 3d and S 2 s spectra and the decrease in the binding energy of C 1 s spectra are attributed to the electron loss of MoS2 and the electron accumulation of Si-CQDs, verifying the synergistic effect of radicals and electron transfer in the MoS2/Si-CQDs system [76].
“Fresh” means before the reaction and “Used” means after the reaction.

On the basis of the above analysis, the mechanisms of catalytic oxidation of naphthenic acids by MoS2/Si-CQDs activated NaPS are proposed as follows and shown in Fig. 11. MoS2/Si-CQDs contains edge active sites belonging to Flower-like MoS2, surface functional groups belonging to Si-CQDs, and unique Mo-O-C electron transport channels. The unique characteristics of the MoS2/Si-CQDs composite alter the electronic environment of NaPS, influencing its electron configuration, weakening the O-O bond, and thereby enhancing its activity. The oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids by MoS2/Si-CQDs system is realized on this basis. On the one hand, for the radical pathway, the “O-O”-weakened S2O8 2− was first hydrolyzed to form HO2 − (Eq. (12)); and then the HO2 − was quickly oxidized by another S2O8 2− via one-electron transfer process, resulting in the generation of SO4 •− and O2 •−(Eq. (10)). At the same time, some of the SO4 •− in the aqueous environment begins to convert to •OH (Eq. (6)). All the generated SO4 •−, •OH and O2 •− would then take part in the oxidation of naphthenic acids. On the other hand, for the non-radical pathway, which is the electron transfer mechanism, The catalyst acts as the electron transfer medium in the oxidation process, and the pollutant contributes electrons to the oxidizer. In the MoS2/Si-CQDs system, a part of naphthenic acids and NaPS were complexed on the surface of MoS2/Si-CQDs，forming an Electron Donor (Naphthenic acids) - Mediator (MoS2/Si-CQDs) - Acceptor (NaPS) ternary system. With the help of MoS2/Si-CQDs, the activated NaPS could directly oxidize naphthenic acids via two-electron conduction (Eq. (13)) without generating free radicals. In MoS2/Si-CQDs system, both radical and non-radical oxidation processes were involved in the reaction and together promote the complete decomposition of naphthenic acids. MoS2/Si-CQDs played a key role as the electron-transferring medium, facilitating the transfer of electrons from naphthenic acids or H2O to NaPS.
(12) S2O8 2− + 2H2O → HO2 − + 2SO4 2− + 3H+
(13) S2O8 2− + 2e− → 2SO4 2−

The degradation activity of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system in complex environments containing a variety of organic compounds was preliminarily tested by targeting QHD-EDW and HZ-EDW, and the 3D-EEMF results are shown in Fig. 12 and Fig S17. As shown in Fig. 12(a), QHD-EDW displayed two main fluorescence peaks (Peak-A1 and Peak-B1) with strong intensity. Peak-A1 was observed at the excitation/emission (EX/EM) of 242–255/361–387 nm, and Peak-B1 was located at 275–302/345–387 nm (EX/EM). Peak-A1 is generally thought to be related to biologically refractory aromatic compounds or certain functional groups (like -OH and NH2) [77,78]. Peak-B1 is related to biodegradable compounds, it is associated with tyrosine-like compounds or tryptophan-like components, which could be polyphenolic compounds [79]. Multiple peaks and high fluorescence intensity indicate that QHD-EDW has a complex composition and high contaminant concentrations, the biodegradability of the wastewater sample was poor [49]. MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system exhibits positive degradation activity in QHD-EDW. After 120 min reaction with MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS (Fig. 12(c)), Peak-A1 and Peak-B1 almost disappeared, indicating that organic pollutants in QHD-EDW were almost completely removed, resulting in significant improvement in biodegradability of the wastewater [49].

Similarly, as shown in Fig S17, the pollutant composition of HZ-EDW is more complex and the content is higher. Therefore, HZ-EDW requires a longer reaction time. In Fig S17(c), Peak-A2 may be associated with tyrosine-like compounds or tryptophan-like components [79], and Peak-B2 may be related to humic acid-like substances [80]. As with QHD-EDW, after 4 h reaction (Fig S17(e)), most of the pollutants in HZ-EDW were removed, and the biodegradability of the wastewater was likewise substantially improved.

In summary, the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system still possesses good degradation activity in real naphthenic acids wastewater and shows positive removal effects on organic pollutants in the wastewater, which contributes to the improvement of biodegradability of difficult-to-biodegrade naphthenic acids petrochemical wastewater.

For catalysts, reusability and stability are key factors in the practical application of the material. The degradation performance of MoS2/Si-CQDs composites was investigated by continuously recycling the catalysts, as shown in Fig. S18. After five cycles, the degradation efficiency of the composites for naphthenic acids still reached over 80 %, which indicates that the composites possess good stability and reusability.

In order to assess its practical feasibility, the impact of the production costs of the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system on the economic indicators was analyzed (Table S5), the main components include the material costs of the synthetic materials, the operating costs of the degradation experiments (energy and reagent costs), and the costs of the degradation experiments. The production cost of the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system is about CNY 21.14 per kg, which is only 14.33 % of the commercial activated carbon (CNY 147.5). The use of inexpensive fructose as a carbon source in this paper reduces the potential cost of energy and reagent costs for wastewater treatment, which are the main operating expenses, but the system exhibits high degradation efficiencies, reduces the need for additional and lowers the long-term operating costs, at the same time high degradation rates increase the biodegradability of the wastewater, which can be improved by reducing downstream biological treatment costs and the environmental penalties associated with untreated waste. In conclusion, the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system proves to be a more cost-effective method for removing naphthenic acids.

In this work, a Flower-like MoS2 was prepared, which was composited with Si-CQDs by hydrothermal method to obtain MoS2/Si-CQDs, and the physicochemical properties of MoS2/Si-CQDs were tested by a series of characterization methods. Si-CQDs and Flower-like MoS2 combine to form MoS2/Si-CQDs through C-O-Mo strong interfacial interaction. The incorporation of Si-CQDs does not change the original lattice structure of Flower-like MoS2, which provides the basis for the enhancement of electron transport rate in MoS2/Si-CQDs.

The oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids by MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system was investigated using MoS2/Si-CQDs as catalysts, NaPS as oxidizing agent, and 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid as model naphthenic acids. The possible reaction mechanisms were also investigated. In the oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids, the MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system showed a significant improvement over the MoS2@NaPS system and Si-CQDs@NaPS system. The MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system degraded naphthenic acids up to more than 99 % in 120 min, and the relationship between the naphthenic acids degradation rate and the reaction temperature was in accordance with the pseudo-primary kinetic equation. Electron paramagnetic resonance and electrochemical experiments shows that there are two pathways of action in MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system: radical action based on reactive oxygen species and non-radical action based on electron transfer. The two pathways work together to effectively promote the oxidative degradation of naphthenic acids. Finally, two typical petrochemical electro desalting wastewaters containing naphthenic acids were selected to test the degradation activity of MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system in complex environments containing a variety of organic compounds. MoS2/Si-CQDs@NaPS system showed good degradation activity in wastewater and improved its biodegradability.

Liu Yize: Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis. Chi Haoran: Validation, Conceptualization. Ren Hongwei: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Zheng Yi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Zhang Qiuya: Software, Methodology, Investigation.
